items.q: Can a business control its external environment?
items.correct: Largely no — PESTLE factors like interest rates, law and demographics lie outside its control, but a firm can anticipate them, lobby, and adapt its strategy.
items.misconception: Well-run businesses can simply decide to remove external threats.
items.random_wrong: Businesses vote each year to set national interest rates.
items.why: Students blur internal and external factors; the external environment is responded to, not controlled.

items.q: Do rising interest rates affect only firms with loans?
items.correct: No — they also cut consumer spending on credit-bought goods, influence exchange rates and change investment plans, hitting demand even for debt-free firms.
items.misconception: If a business has no borrowing, interest rates are irrelevant to it.
items.random_wrong: Interest rates only apply to savings accounts.
items.why: Students trace only the direct loan-cost channel and miss the demand-side effects.

items.q: Is a growing economy good news for every business?
items.correct: Mostly but not all — sellers of inferior goods and discount retailers can lose customers as incomes rise, and booms bring higher wages, costs and interest rates.
items.misconception: Economic growth benefits all businesses equally.
items.random_wrong: Economic growth above 5% a year is illegal.
items.why: Income elasticity means some products lose out in booms — a classic evaluation twist.

items.q: Rising interest rates fall under which PESTLE letter?
items.why: It's Economic. “Political” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: New data protection law (GDPR) is primarily which letter?
items.why: Correct answer: Legal. “Social” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.
Exact answer: 6. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
